ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai grilled chicken rice; closest defensible match is the generic chicken rice dish class (FOODON_03311069).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Thai grilled chicken rice; A0FJP (chicken meat dish) is the closest defensible category-level anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific composite dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Thai grilled chicken rice typically comprises approximately 200 g of steamed jasmine rice and 150 g of grilled chicken pieces with dipping sauce, totalling approximately 350 g.

nutrient_source.title: Thai Grilled Chicken Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 for all per_100g fields. Values are preserved as supplied. The dish is listed in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as a composite rice-based dish. The HPB entry reports core macros, sugar, dietary fibre, sodium, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, and cholesterol only; fatty acid fractions, vitamins, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture are not individually reported in this entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Jasmine rice (white, cooked) as analogous GI anchor for jasmine-rice-dominant composite dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai grilled chicken rice as a composite dish. The GI of 72 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white jasmine rice (cooked), which constitutes the dominant carbohydrate component (~57% of total weight). The grilled chicken component contributes negligible carbohydrate and does not materially alter the composite GI. Glycemic load calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (72 × 52.92) / 100 ≈ 38.1, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 15.12 × 3.5 = 52.92 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 324 mg/100 g with low potassium offset (182 mg/100 g) and low dietary fibre (1.0 g/100 g); a full 350 g serving delivers approximately 1,134 mg sodium, representing over 49% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced marinade (fish sauce, oyster sauce, soy sauce), ask for chilli dipping sauce on the side or avoid it entirely, and limit portion size to reduce total sodium intake per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White jasmine rice is the dominant carbohydrate source with a high GI of approximately 72; a standard 350 g serving delivers approximately 52.9 g carbohydrate with only 3.5 g dietary fibre, producing a high glycemic load of ~38.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should substitute brown rice or reduce the rice portion to lower glycemic load, monitor total carbohydrate per serving, and request sugar-free or reduced-sugar chilli dipping sauce to avoid additional glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 324 mg/100 g is elevated and contributes to cardiovascular risk; fat is moderate at 4.0 g/100 g; cholesterol is low at 13 mg/100 g; grilled preparation avoids additional saturated fat from frying.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to manage sodium intake, request reduced-sodium marinade, and note that the grilled (non-fried) preparation and lean chicken are relatively favourable compared to fried alternatives, though the high-sodium condiments remain a concern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Grilled preparation avoids AGE generation; turmeric (curcumin), lemongrass, and galangal in the marinade provide evidence-based anti-inflammatory polyphenols; however, chilli dipping sauce contains nightshade vegetables which may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the anti-inflammatory marinade ingredients but should monitor tolerance to nightshade-containing chilli dipping sauce and request reduced fish sauce and oyster sauce to limit sodium-driven tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Grilled Chicken Rice (ข้าวไก่ย่าง, Khao Kai Yang) is a popular Thai dish consisting of marinated grilled chicken served alongside steamed jasmine rice, typically accompanied by a chilli dipping sauce. The chicken is marinated in a blend of lemongrass, galangal, turmeric, garlic, fish sauce, oyster sauce, and soy sauce before being grilled over charcoal or a gas grill, producing a smoky char and deeply aromatic flavour. The jasmine rice is steamed plain and provides the starchy base of the meal. The dish is widely consumed in Thailand and is also a common hawker and food court offering in Singapore, where it is sold under names such as 'R2 Thai Grilled Chicken Rice'. Per 100 g, the composite dish provides 116 kcal, 4.96 g protein, 4.0 g fat, 15.12 g carbohydrate, 1.56 g sugar, 1.0 g dietary fibre, and 324 mg sodium. The high sodium content (324 mg/100 g) is a primary clinical concern for individuals with hypertension and cardiovascular disease, as a full serving can deliver over 1,100 mg sodium. The white jasmine rice base carries a high glycemic index (~72) and produces a high glycemic load per serving (~38), making portion control critical for individuals with type 2 diabetes. The grilled preparation method avoids the additional saturated fat and advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) associated with deep-frying, and the turmeric and lemongrass in the marinade contribute anti-inflammatory polyphenols relevant to conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. The dish is dairy-free and generally halal-suitable when prepared with halal-certified condiments.

confidence_reason: All per_100g nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95, which is an approved primary regional source for Singapore-served dishes. The input record carries 'canonical' provenance and 'reviewed' review status. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using white jasmine rice as the analogous anchor, which is the dominant carbohydrate component of the dish.

GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white jasmine rice (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for this composite dish exists in approved sources. Glycemic load per serving calculated at 350 g serving size: GL = (72 × 52.92) / 100 ≈ 38.1. The dish is sold in Singapore under the brand/menu name 'R2 Thai Grilled Chicken Rice'; the canonical food name has been normalised to 'Thai Grilled Chicken Rice'. Halal suitability is conditional on use of halal-certified fish sauce, oyster sauce, and soy sauce.
